title: Unable to use host pid inside container
body:
### Issue Description

When I run rootful podman with privileged option, I am unable to pass pid=host while starting the container.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Create a docker image with the following template:
```
FROM ubuntu22.04 AS base

RUN apt-get -qqy update && apt-get -qqy install --no-install-recommends \
    systemd systemd-sysv && \
    apt-get install -y \
    vim git curl wget net-tools iputils-ping build-essential jq tmux htop tree dnsutils libpam-systemd locales netcat \
    cmake rsync cron openssh-server openssh-client sudo nvidia-container-toolkit software-properties-common nvtop unzip \
    openssl gnupg2 \
    && rm -rf /var/cache/apt/*

RUN systemctl enable ssh.service

# Start the systemd Init service by default
CMD [ "/sbin/init" ]
```
2.  `sudo podman run --name test --privileged --systemd always --pid host --device nvidia.com/gpu=all --device /dev/net/tun:/dev/net/tun --replace -d <image>`



### Describe the results you received

It fails to start the container with the following error:

`Running in chroot, ignoring request.`

It seems like if I have `/sbin/init` as my default command for my container, this is causing issue to pass the host pid to the container.

### Describe the results you expected

I expected to get the podman container running

comments.body:
I don't know if there is anything the Podman team can do for you here; it looks like the container is starting correctly, and it's systemd that takes exception to `--pid=host` (which makes some sense, I think systemd absolutely wants to be PID 1, even in a container). We could potentially makes `--pid=host` conflict with `--systemd=always`, I suppose?

If you are looking for assistance getting the container running in this configuration, though, a systemd bug will be your best bet.

Is there any workaround for this configuration? 
When I run `sudo podman run --name test --privileged --pid host --replace -d <image>`, it fails with error `Running in chroot, ignoring request.`

no, there is no workaround and there is nothing podman can do about it.

systemd just refuses to work with a PID != 1.  That makes no sense anyway since PID=1 has a special meaning, systemd can't work well if it is not running with PID 1.
